text: The surgeon carefully retracts the omentum from the inflamed appendix, revealing the localized phlegmon at the base of the cecum without any signs of perforation spread.
The surgeon carefully retracts the omentum away from the appendix to improve visibility and access for further dissection.
The video shows the omentum surrounding the inflamed appendix, acting as a barrier to contain the localized phlegmon without any visible spread of perforation throughout the abdomen.

The surgeon carefully navigates from known to unknown areas to reach the base of the cecum where the appendix junction is visible. The yellow anti-mesenteric fat on the terminal ileum is observed above the ligature, and the base of the appendix is identified.

The surgeon carefully navigates from known to unknown areas to reach the base of the cecum at the junction with the appendix. The yellow fat visible above the ligature is identified as the anti-mesenteric fat on the terminal ileum.
The surgeon identifies the base of the appendix and maneuvers to expose it further. The appendix is visibly folded, displaying a curlicue appearance as the surrounding tissues are gently unfolded.

The surgeon observes the curlicue shape of the folded appendix and mobilizes the terminal ileum and cecum to improve access and angles for dissection. The ureter is checked as a precaution before proceeding with this maneuver.

The surgeon mobilizes the terminal ileum and cecum to improve access and maneuverability of the appendix, ensuring the ureter is checked beforehand. This maneuver allows for better angles for dissection.

The surgeon identifies the wall of the cecum, which is now more mobile, facilitating blunt dissection. The mesoappendix is located above the terminal ileal fat, and the surgeon prepares to dissect through this area, confirming the folded position of the appendix with its base visible.

The surgeon identifies the wall of the cecum, noting increased mobility for blunt dissection. The mesoappendix is located above the terminal ileal fat, and the surgeon prepares to dissect through it, confirming the folded position of the appendix with its base visible.

The surgeon divides the base of the appendix and proceeds to dissect through the mesoappendix. Pus is visibly draining from the abscess located in the mesentery of the appendix. The dissection reveals the plane between the anti-mesenteric fat of the terminal ileum and the mesoappendix, allowing the surgeon to continue clearing the mesentery.

The surgeon divides the base of the appendix and enters the phlegmon, with visible pus draining from the abscess located in the mesentery to the appendix. A small incision helps access the plane between the anti-mesenteric fat of the terminal ileum and the mesoappendix, allowing further dissection through the mesentery.

The surgeon manipulates the mesoappendix, revealing the base of the appendix, which appears thinned out. The surgeon uses a ligature to grasp the tissue, exposing the last portion of the mesoappendix or phlegmon on the lateral aspect. The appendix is elevated, and the surgeon debates the next step, opting to use a stapler to transect the tissue.

The surgeon manipulates the mesoappendix, revealing the thinned-out base of the appendix. Using a ligature, they attempt to grasp the structure, exposing the last portion of the mesoappendix or phlegmon on the lateral side. The appendix is elevated, and the surgeon considers transecting it with the ligature before opting to use a stapler.
The surgeon examines the surgical site, considering the use of a stapler versus over-sewing the area. A small nub is visible, and the surgeon decides to over-sew it using figure-of-eight sutures.
The surgeon prepares to over-sew the stump of the appendix using a 2-0 Vicryl suture, taking large bites beneath the stump after cleaning the area.
The surgeon uses a 2-0 Vicryl suture to take large bites underneath the stump of the appendix, aiming to bunch up the surrounding tissue with multiple stitches.
The surgeon uses a long suture to take large bites, bunching up the tissue around the stump of the appendix.
The surgeon performs an omentopexy by taking a large bite of the omentum and securing it to the lateral border of the appendix stump, ensuring it remains in place for effective sealing.
The surgeon performs an omentopexy by taking a large bite of the omentum and securing it to the lateral border of the appendix stump, ensuring it remains in place to seal the area.
